notes.section_title: 1. The First World War and Its Impact

content.text: The years after 1919 saw the national movement spreading to new areas and social groups. The First World War created a new economic and political situation by leading to a huge increase in defence expenditure, financed by war loans and increasing taxes.

content.title: Impact of the War
items: Customs duties were raised and income tax was introduced.
Prices doubled between 1913 and 1918, leading to extreme hardship for common people.
Forced recruitment in rural areas for the British army caused widespread anger.
Crops failed in 1918-19 and 1920-21, resulting in acute food shortages.
An influenza epidemic led to the death of 12 to 13 million people (Census of 1921).

content.text: Mahatma Gandhi returned to India in January 1915 from South Africa, where he had successfully fought the racist regime with a novel method of mass agitation called satyagraha.

content.title: What is Satyagraha?
content.text: The idea of satyagraha emphasised the power of truth and the need to search for truth. It suggested that if the cause was true and the struggle was against injustice, then physical force was not necessary. A satyagrahi could win the battle through non-violence by appealing to the conscience of the oppressor.

content.title: Early Satyagraha Movements
items: 1917: Champaran (Bihar) – To inspire peasants against the oppressive plantation system.
1917: Kheda (Gujarat) – To support peasants affected by crop failure and plague, demanding revenue relaxation.

notes.section_title: 3. The Rowlatt Act and Jallianwalla Bagh

content.text: The Rowlatt Act was hurriedly passed by the Imperial Legislative Council in 1919 despite Indian opposition. it gave the government enormous powers to repress political activities and allowed detention of political prisoners without trial for two years.

Gandhiji started a nationwide satyagraha with a hartal on 6 April. Rallies were organised, and shops were closed down. the British administration clamped down on nationalists, barring Gandhi from Delhi and arresting local leaders in Amritsar.

On 13 April 1919, a large crowd gathered at Jallianwalla Bagh; some to protest and others to attend the Baisakhi fair. General Dyer entered, blocked the exits, and opened fire on the crowd, killing hundreds.

content.title: General Dyer's Objective
content.text: Dyer declared later that his object was to 'produce a moral effect' and create a feeling of terror and awe in the minds of satyagrahis.
As news spread, strikes and clashes with police broke out. the government responded with brutal repression, forcing satyagrahis to rub their noses on the ground and crawl on streets.

Gandhiji felt a broad-based movement was impossible without bringing Hindus and Muslims together. He took up the Khilafat issue — the defense of the Ottoman Emperor (Khalifa) after Turkey's defeat in WWI.

content.title: Khilafat Committee
content.text: Formed in Bombay in March 1919 by Muhammad Ali and Shaukat Ali. Gandhiji convinced Congress leaders at the Calcutta session (Sept 1920) to start a Non-Cooperation movement in support of Khilafat and swaraj.

In his book 'Hind Swaraj' (1909), Gandhiji declared that British rule survived only because of Indian cooperation. the movement was to unfold in stages: surrendering titles, boycotting civil services, army, police, courts, schools, and foreign goods.
After intense debate within the Congress, the Non-Cooperation programme was adopted at the Nagpur session in December 1920.
Middle-class participation led the movement. Students left schools, and lawyers gave up practices. Economic boycott was significant: import of foreign cloth halved between 1921 and 1922.

content.title: Limitations in Cities
content.text: The movement slowed down because Khadi was more expensive than mass-produced mill cloth, and alternative Indian institutions were slow to develop.
In Awadh, Baba Ramchandra led peasants against talukdars and landlords. the Oudh Kisan Sabha was set up in 1920. However, the movement turned violent, which unhappy the Congress leadership.

content.title: Tribal Struggles
content.text: In the Gudem Hills of Andhra Pradesh, Alluri Sitaram Raju led a militant guerrilla movement against forest laws. inspired by Gandhi but believing in the use of force, he became a folk hero.
For workers in Assam, swaraj meant the right to move freely. they defied the Inland Emigration Act of 1859 and left tea gardens, believing 'Gandhi Raj' was coming.
Gandhiji withdrew the movement in February 1922 after the Chauri Chaura incident. Within Congress, C.R. Das and Motilal Nehru formed the Swaraj Party to participate in council elections.

content.title: Factors Shaping Politics
items: Worldwide economic depression: Agricultural prices collapsed after 1930.
Simon Commission (1928): Set up to suggest constitutional changes but had no Indian members. Greets with 'Go back Simon'.
Lahore Congress (1929): Under Jawaharlal Nehru, demand for 'Purna Swaraj' (Full Independence) was formalised.

notes.section_title: 7. The Salt March and Civil Disobedience

content.text: Gandhiji sent 11 demands to Viceroy Irwin, including the abolition of salt tax. Salt was consumed by all, and the tax on it revealed the oppressive face of British rule.

content.title: Dandi March
content.text: Gandhiji started the march with 78 volunteers from Sabarmati to Dandi (240 miles). On 6 April 1930, he reached Dandi and broke the salt law by boiling sea water.
Unlike NCM, users were now asked to break colonial laws. Thousands participated; government responded with arrests (including Abdul Ghaffar Khan and Gandhi) and brutal repression.

content.title: Gandhi-Irwin Pact (1931)
content.text: Gandhiji called off the movement and agreed to attend the Second Round Table Conference in London. When it failed, he relaunched the movement in 1932, but it lost momentum by 1934.

content.title: Participants of Civil Disobedience
rows: Struggle against high revenue; hit by depression.
Reduction of revenue and remission of unpaid rent.
Protection against imports; end of colonial restrictions.
Improvement in wages and working conditions (limited participation).
Sacred duty to the nation; large scale participation in marches/picketing.

content.text: Congress long ignored 'untouchables' (dalits). Gandhiji called them 'Harijan' and worked for their dignity. Dr. B.R. Ambedkar demanded separate electorates and formed the Depressed Classes Association (1930).

content.title: Poona Pact (Sept 1932)
content.text: After Gandhiji's fast unto death, Ambedkar accepted common electorates with reserved seats for Depressed Classes in provincial and central councils.
Many Muslim organisations were lukewarm due to alienation after the Khilafat-NCM decline. Distrust grew as Congress became associated with Hindu groups like the Mahasabha.

content.title: Muhammad Iqbal's View
content.text: In 1930, Iqbal reiterated the need for separate electorates to safeguard Muslim political interests, providing justification for the future Pakistan demand.
Nationalism captured imagination through history, fiction, folklore, and symbols.

content.title: Key Cultural Elements
items: Bharat Mata: Identity visually symbolised; first created by Bankim Chandra Chattopadhyay (Vande Mataram) and later Abanindranath Tagore.
Folklore: Movement to revive Indian folklore led by Rabindranath Tagore and Natesa Sastri (Folklore of Southern India).
Icons and Symbols: Tricolour flags designed in Bengal (Swadeshi) and by Gandhiji (Swaraj flag with spinning wheel).
Reinterpretation of History: Writing about great ancient achievements to instill pride and counter British views of India as primitive.

content.text: गांधीजी launched the movement after the failure of the Cripps Mission during WWII. the AICC passed the 'Quit India' resolution in Bombay on 8 August 1942.

content.title: Do or Die
content.text: Gandhiji's famous speech 'Do or Die' inspired a massive movement involving students, workers, and peasants. despite force, it marked a decisive stage in the struggle for freedom.

glossary.definition: A process by which the colonial state forced people to join the army.
A novel method of mass agitation based on truth and non-violence, as developed by Mahatma Gandhi.
A 1919 act that allowed the government to detain political prisoners without trial for up to two years.
The refusal to deal and associate with people, or participate in activities, or buy and use things; usually a form of protest.
A form of demonstration by which people block the entrance to a shop, factory or office.
Labour that villagers were forced to contribute without any payment.
An act that prevented plantation workers from leaving tea gardens without permission.
The Simon Commission, set up in 1928 to suggest constitutional changes in India.
The demand for complete independence, formulated at the 1929 Lahore Congress.
A movement that refused to cooperate with and also to break colonial laws.
An agreement in March 1931 where Gandhi agreed to stop the Civil Disobedience movement and attend the Round Table Conference.
A 1932 agreement giving reserved seats to Depressed Classes in legislative councils but within a general electorate.
A belief system based on the perceived antagonistic interests of different religious communities.
The visual personification of India as a mother figure.

summary_points: Modern nationalism in India emerged as a response to anti-colonial struggle, which provided a shared bond among diverse groups.
Mahatma Gandhi introduced the concept of Satyagraha, emphasizing non-violence and soul-force, starting with local movements in Champaran and Kheda.
The 1919 Rowlatt Act and the Jallianwalla Bagh massacre turned the national struggle into a mass movement.
The Non-Cooperation-Khilafat movement (1921-22) saw broad participation but was called off after the Chauri Chaura incident.
The 1929 Lahore Congress's demand for 'Purna Swaraj' led to the Salt March and the Civil Disobedience Movement in 1930.
The movement was characterized by the breaking of salt laws and widespread boycotts, though it had limits regarding Dalit and Muslim participation.
The Poona Pact of 1932 resolved tensions between Gandhi and Ambedkar regarding representation for Depressed Classes.
Nationalism was forged not just through political struggle but also icons, folklore, flags, and the reinterpretation of history.
The 1942 Quit India Movement represented the peak of mass resistance with the slogan 'Do or Die', leading towards final independence.
